item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is cute and I had a big smile imagining some cruel saboteur programming in commands for a disease like "odorless, tasteless, colorless, symptomless malaria (airborne)" or "nuclear projectile pustules (super keter)"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Belatedly fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one.

SCP-1153-2 are a collection of five (as of ██/██/████) laptop computers of various makes and models

That should be "SCP-1153-2 is a collection" I believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. Makes me wonder like hell who the hell made this? And it's a terribly relevant question considering the authors are still updating the program…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just thought that "some bodies appear identical" seemed a little less than clinical. You could say "three pairs of bodies" and just cut down on the number for the first (though it would kind of lessen the effect). I don't know, I think you could include a little bit of that data, though I agree having all of it would be unwieldy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had actually specified that on the first draft I came up with (before the one I put up in sandbox), but it just became unwieldy and confusing. So here comes some ultimately pointless minutiae about this item!

There are three bodies that appear to be the same male, mid thirties; two who appear to be the same female, early twenties; two who appear to be the same male, about ten years old. All the others are unique (as far as the Foundation knows, at least).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't include any specific SCP items because crosslinking is largely prohibited.

They have checked it, and they know what is on the program. The reader, however, doesn't. For functional purposes, one can assume the commands include every disease on Foundation record, seeing as it doesn't affect the actually point of the article whether or not a particular disease is on there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting question, and not one I'd considered. I'm going to say that tissue samples disappear at the onset of a Wipe command, but develop diseases developed by the main body unti lthe wipe is given.

I just had a horrible thought: what if someone got a transplant from a healthy SCP-1153 instance, and then week or two later a Wipe was executed? Their organs would just disappear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is excellent, and so incredibly useful. I'm glad that the diseases are still communicable as normal, to keep this from being too useful.

How many of the bodies are similar?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The US military only started getting vaccinated again in 2002, so earlier versions of this SCP could have still affected them. The vaccinations in the 60-7-'s were never designed to last a lifetime-the idea was to immunize the population and get rid of the reservoir of smallpox (also called herd immunity). Most people would not be immune at this point, and, with a large enough exposure, a new outbreak of disease could break through people's immunizations, setting off an epidemic.

I wouldn't call it a grievous error, just an error. : )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just… how?

Hint: Read 1025 entry carefully, again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll atone for that grievous factual error by removing it. Sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Dr. Schubert.

I have to point out that all people in Taiwan who were born in the 70s received their cowpox vaccines (I had two since the first one didn't grant me the smallpox immunity) and therefore, are immune to smallpox.

Though I upvoted, but I think you need to fix that a little bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this. The SCP is dangerous enough without being able to work by itself, and "it starts doing dangerous things when left alone" is probably the most overused way to add danger to an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for people-ports and possible insidious data collection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this when you posted it in sandbox, and I like it now even more. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very interesting. The perfect autopsy/disease study models. A question: what happens to tissue samples taken from the bodies? Do these disappear when the Wipe command is given, or do they remain? If they remain, do they get whatever disease is programmed next? Could they be used for transplants?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"as well as extinct diseases such as smallpox to which no modern human possesses any resistance to. "

LIES. Any soldier in the US military would have a resistance to smallpox, as well as a sizable part of the population which have received their vaccine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too similar to SCP-1025 for my tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I told ya I would upvote it, and I did. Love it, love everything about it. Makes me think of the time I was thinking about going to med school.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, though that might not be saying much. I wouldn't downvote if it left, either.

item.title: Just a quick question
item.content:encoded: "The computer will not require power during this process, for reasons Foundation personnel are currently able to identify." should probably be "The computer will not require power during this process, for reasons Foundation personnel are currently not able to identify." or somesuch?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit I mostly added that to throw in a sense of danger to the item, which in retrospect was probably misguided. I'll leave it for a little while to see what other people think, but I'll be happy to remove it.

EDIT: That paragraph has been removed. Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, that part feels tacked on compared to the rest. Upvoted, still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like the sixth paragraph of the description. The rest is good enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. The idea clicks for me. +1.
Also, there should be something about SCP-008 in there. Seems pretty high priority to check in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My second SCP. Hopefully, it won't ruin the good impression I put across with my first one. Feel free to tear it apart.
